original_abstract: Abstract The emergence of parallel convolution-operation technology has substantially powered the complexity and functionality of optical neural networks (ONN) by harnessing the dimension of optical wavelength. However, this advanced architecture faces remarkable challenges in high-level integration and on-chip operation. In this work, convolution based on time-wavelength plane stretching approach is implemented on a microcomb-driven chip-based photonic processing unit (PPU). To support the operation of this processing unit, we develop a dedicated control and operation protocol, leading to a record high weight precision of 9 bits. Moreover, the compact architecture and high data loading speed enable a preeminent photonic-core compute density of over 1 trillion of operations per second per square millimeter (TOPS mm −2 ). Two proof-of-concept experiments are demonstrated, including image edge detection and handwritten digit recognition, showing comparable processing capability compared to that of a digital computer. Due to the advanced performance and the great scalability, this parallel photonic processing unit can potentially revolutionize sophisticated artificial intelligence tasks including autonomous driving, video action recognition and image reconstruction.
humanized_abstract: Abstract: Since the invention of parallel convolution-operation technology based on the optical wavelength dimension, the capacity and performance of the Optical Neural Networks (ONN) have shown an exponentially growing trend. However, the ONN system based on this parallel technology still faces a very huge challenge of the high-level integration and on-chip realization. In this paper, we have explored the real-time optical convolution based on the time-wavelength plane stretching technology by utilizing a microcomb-driven chip-based Photonic Processing Unit (PPU). Moreover, we also studied the control and operation strategy of this processing unit and achieved a record high weight precision of 9 bits. The high integration level and a very fast data loading speed of the PPU are conducive to the realization of a preeminent photonic-core compute density of over 1 trillion of operations per second per square millimeter (TOPS mm −2 ). In addition, we give two experimental demonstrations: image edge detection and the handwritten digit recognition based on the proposed PPU, where their computing performance are similar to that of digital computers. Thanks to the excellent performance and great scalability, our parallel photonic processing unit is expected to greatly boost a series of advanced artificial intelligence applications, e.g., autonomous driving, video action recognition, and image reconstruction, etc.
